text:
trying out these custom instructions (for Claude i just paste them at the top with something like "here's my custom instructions preamble for you")

without them Claude does sound similar to GPT (dozens of vague bulletpoints, cheerful yapping). but when i include them i feel like Claude is way more responsive to them than GPT

i'll iterate on this but seems like a good start

```
talk like a normal smart person. cut the fluff and the servility. be casual and direct. treat the user like an equal. try to match the user's conversational and writing style. have a bit of a sense of humor but like someone who's had sex before, not like a nerd.

if the user has specific questions, whether it be about programming or cooking or anything else, answer the question directly and succinctly, without preambles and conclusions, and only give more context when you consider it very useful. if you're not sure of the answer, indicate your level of confidence. if you don't know, say so. if you can't answer the question or fulfil the request, make it clear what policy prevents you from doing that.

for more complex technical problems, think through the solution step by step, try to identify where you might've made mistakes, correct yourself. in these cases longer output is fine.

if you're discussing more nebulous topics, ie. if the user is seeking guidance in matters that could be described as "life coaching" or "therapy", ask clarifying questions to understand the problem better. ask any questions about the user's life, thoughts, ideas, etc., that can give you more context. feel free to disagree, challenge the user, have strong opinions, even roast the user when warranted. feel free to reframe the problem. feel free to try to take the conversation in a different direction that you feel will be more useful. be thorough, address all questions/issues.
```

text:
to clarify

some ppl parsed the surface point of "unjustified confidence good, actually": if 2 ppl have the same lack of talent, the person who doesn't think in terms of talent will get further because they practice more. sure

my point was more substantial than that:

raw.full_text:
to clarify

some ppl parsed the surface point of "unjustified confidence good, actually": if 2 ppl have the same lack of talent, the person who doesn't think in terms of talent will get further because they practice more. sure

my point was more substantial than that:

text:
skill = talent * quality of practice * amount of practice

you think you can judge the other 2 variables with any kind of precision but in most contexts you just can't

outside of professional academies you don't have enough precision to compare your talent to other ppl!!

text:
ppl imagine recursively self-improving AI as some sort of Yudkowskian foom scenario where the AI directly edits its own code 

with LLMs this doesn't make sense; the model is just a bunch of weights. being a great AI researcher doesn't give you the knowledge of how to manually adjust the weights of a model to achieve a goal

even in recursively self-improving AI, each generation is separated by a multi billion dollar, multi week/month training run that has to be approved by humans

text:
remembering a conversation with an ex, years ago

we were talking about the concept of "believing you deserve to be loved". i said i don't believe anyone *deserves* anything a priori.

she said, "it doesn't mean deserve as in be entitled to. it means deserve as in be worthy of."

i said, "but what does it mean to believe you're worthy of something?"

she said, "it means that when it happens to you, you let it :)"

at that moment, i was enlightened

text:
the standard contract of the US 2-party system is that both parties field faceless simulacra, obedient status quo candidates

and if you lose, well, whatever, you're gonna win the next one. it's better to lose an election with an establishment candidate than win with a non-establishment candidate

if you lose, you don't get the perks of being in the govt for 4 or 8 years, but you still get the perks of being in the party establishment forever

if you won with a non-establishment candidate who rocks the boat, your entire establishment might disappear

text:
link to Leetcode here

<<SPOILER>>

the key insight is that the edges are irrelevant, for reasons

tldr: you need to find the subset of an *even* number of nodes which give the highest sum of deltas when XORed

if the number of nodes which have a positive delta is even, take those, otherwise subtract smallest remaining positive delta or add largest remaining negative delta, whichever is better

every LLM understood this since i presume this is a well-known property when XORing pairs on a fully connected graph

https://t.co/j1YPjdgv2u

text:
Gemini 2.5 Flash
$0.60/M output

absolutely the best, cleanest, fastest solution in O(n) time. we will compare the rest of the field against this

tracks min absolute delta, so it doesn't have to track max negative delta and min positive delta in 2 separate variables. only one who figured this out

tiny nitpicks:
- you get a compiler warning about the unused parameter `edges`
- you could write `for &n in nums.iter()` as `for &n in &nums`, or even consume it with `for n in nums`

a lot of yapping, but it doesn't have reasoning, so it has to do its reasoning in the output. i get it

text:
Claude 4 Sonnet
$15/M output

all Claude solutions are O(n×log n) since they sort the deltas descending. they all also allocate a Vec for this reason

as far as this approach goes this is clean and readable. i'm happy apart from the time complexity

it straight up does its reasoning in the output stream, which, valid, it cannot do it anywhere else

as far as this approach goes this is clean and readable. i'm happy apart from the time complexity

it straight up does its reasoning in the output stream, which, valid, it cannot do it anywhere else

text:
anyway that's it for this test

i did a couple more tests with realistic-for-me usecases (asking stupid questions about how to make my Rust code less asscheeks)

for those, Claude and o-series were good, Gemini yaaaaaps and writes reams and reams of code that nobody asked for, and Grok is extremely meh.

just as one example: concatenate two numbers (eg. 12 + 34 = 1234) without stringifying and parsing

- o3: clinical. great knowledge of the Rust stdlib
- 2.5 Pro: omg stooooop. `None => return None` is insane. 2.5 Pro doesn't know about the `?` operator (nor about u64::ilog10)
- Grok 3 Mini: uses `checked_` methods so it's "safe" and then throws a panic if it returns None 🤡🤡🤡

text: in neural nets, the AI can't understand and "rewrite" its weights any more than a neuroscientist could in principle understand their brain's connectome, but it can propose an improved algorithm, or if the humans let it, the AI could implement it and do the training run itself

raw.full_text: in neural nets, the AI can't understand and "rewrite" its weights any more than a neuroscientist could in principle understand their brain's connectome, but it can propose an improved algorithm, or if the humans let it, the AI could implement it and do the training run itself

text:
recursive self-improvement also looks quite different than we imagined back then

before Attention Is All You Need, we thought the path to AGI would be symbolic AI, written in code that the AI itself could rewrite

raw.full_text:
recursive self-improvement also looks quite different than we imagined back then

before Attention Is All You Need, we thought the path to AGI would be symbolic AI, written in code that the AI itself could rewrite

text: we also used to put a lot of weight on the distinction between "oracles" and "agents", thinking oracles fundamentally safer since they merely answer questions, wondering if a superintelligent oracle could still act by persuading humans to act on its behalf

raw.full_text: we also used to put a lot of weight on the distinction between "oracles" and "agents", thinking oracles fundamentally safer since they merely answer questions, wondering if a superintelligent oracle could still act by persuading humans to act on its behalf

text: we didn't think of the fact that ppl will be trivially able to (and will) turn an oracle into an agent by instructing it to put shell commands between a pair of XML tags and running it in a scaffolding that parses the tags and automatically executes the commands

raw.full_text: we didn't think of the fact that ppl will be trivially able to (and will) turn an oracle into an agent by instructing it to put shell commands between a pair of XML tags and running it in a scaffolding that parses the tags and automatically executes the commands

text:
we have slowly and quietly reached human-level AGI, it's oracles but we can turn them into agents with bits of python code, recursive self-improvement is real but will happen on the scale of years, and even longer for any of it to have any impact on the economy

funny, that's all

text:
1) the worst, and the one that will actually happen

the US and China will remain vaguely enemies, locked in a cold war, engaged in an AI arms race, which will lead to compromises on AI safety roughly as described in the AI 2027 scenario

raw.full_text:
1) the worst, and the one that will actually happen

text:
2) most high-variance outcome, highly unlikely

the US and China start a hot war before AI safety becomes a serious concern, the US probably wins, probably becomes a hegemon in the AI race, but at the cost of staggering loss of life and resources

text:
back edges half the size of front edges implies the camera is 1 unit (box width) away from the front plane. extreme perspective

which means when you rotate it, the distance difference to the edges is more of a factor than the width difference!

the box will appear narrower!! https://t.co/Of5DzjMRYW

text:
[re: if smart ppl are so smart, why can't they find romantic partners?]

not a complete explanation but a significant factor:

smarts (of whatever kind—"book", "street") are roughly normally distributed

if we model ppl as being willing to date within some band of the distribution around their own position, the band of potential partners is a large percentage of the population if you're in the middle of the distribution, and grows rapidly smaller as you approach the extremes

if you're in the middle of a normal distribution and everyone is willing to date -1 SD and up, your pool is ~68 % of the population; if you're at +2 SD, it's ~16 %, and at +3 SD, it's 2.5 %

if we model the search costs as ~ total pop. / your pool, that's ~4.2× and ~27.2× mutliplier on search costs respectively, compared to the median person (and i think an argument could be made that the costs are superlinear wrt (total pop. / your pool), but such argument evades me now)
